lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market automotive glass businesses providing repair, replacement, tinting, mobile service, and required camera or driver-assistance recalibration to consumers, fleets, and insurers through stand-alone shops or technician routes.
lens.excluded_activities: Building-glass contractors, automotive body shops whose glass work is incidental, vehicle dealers and fleet operators performing captive work, glass manufacturing or wholesale distribution, consumer-only do-it-yourself products, shells, non-control vehicles, and owner-dependent hobby operations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is earned per repair, replacement, tint, or recalibration job, paid directly by vehicle owners, commercial fleets, or insurers. The model combines appointment and claims coordination, vehicle-specific glass sourcing, mobile routing or shop capacity, physical installation, cure and leak checks, and increasingly safety-system calibration and documentation.

a.bridge: BLS reports broad NAICS 811120 with 12.64% of employment in office and administrative support, 7.08% in business and financial operations, 6.30% in management, 3.46% in sales, and 50.32% in installation, maintenance, and repair. I map intake, estimating, insurance documentation, parts matching, appointment and route scheduling, customer communication, billing, and record review to AI-exposed tasks, while leaving removal, frame preparation, adhesive application, glass seating, leak inspection, tint installation, and safety-critical calibration execution largely unexposed. The five-digit proxy is dominated by body and paint work, likely understating the standardized intake and mobile-routing share of glass shops but also overstating claims-estimating work; the range covers both directions.
a.rationale: Glass shops have a material information and coordination layer, but most service-delivery time remains physical, vehicle-specific, and safety-sensitive.
a.change_evidence: An exact 811122 study of hours by claims intake, quoting, parts search, routing, installation, calibration, quality control, and administration would materially change the exposure estimate.
caveats: Occupation shares are not direct task-hour shares and exclude self-employed workers.
ADAS tools can automate diagnostics and documentation without automating the accountable calibration procedure.

rho.rationale: Online intake, VIN and part matching, insurer documentation, customer messaging, appointment optimization, route planning, and technician-assistance tools can be integrated within five years. Adoption is slowed by fragmented operators, insurer interfaces, inconsistent vehicle data, mobile connectivity, calibration equipment, manufacturer-specific procedures, liability review, and limited management capacity.
rho.change_evidence: Shop-level cohorts showing deployment, daily active use, override rates, integration time, and measured labor or cycle-time effects for AI-assisted glass workflows would materially change implementation.
caveats: Buying claims or scheduling software is not equivalent to sustained AI-enabled workflow use.
Static and dynamic calibration requirements differ across vehicles and can restrict standardized deployment.

e.rationale: The exact NAICS code is a coherent outsourced vehicle service, so most economically material stand-alone firms fit the operating lens. The estimate removes captive dealer or fleet work, body shops classified elsewhere, tint-focused microbusinesses, highly owner-dependent mobile operations, and firms outside the target earnings band. The supplied firm count is only an estimate because it is margin-bridged rather than based on observed exact-industry EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue mix, ownership, EBITDA, insurer concentration, stand-alone status, and enterprise-to-establishment links would materially revise eligibility.
caveats: Multi-location and mobile operators make establishment-to-firm conversion uncertain.
Insurer network dependence, technician concentration, and owner relationships can impair practical transferability.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners intended to sell, transfer, or go public within five years. I haircut that rate for plans that do not close, family or employee transfers that do not create a qualifying control opportunity, and glass businesses outside the target lens. The broad survey can overstate closed external sales because it measures intentions, but it may understate opportunity if insurer-network scale and calibration investment accelerate consolidation.
s5.rationale: Qualifying external control transfers should be materially less common than stated succession intentions, particularly within the small supplied target-firm population.
s5.change_evidence: Exact-industry broker listings, closed acquisitions, owner-age distributions, and external-versus-internal transfer outcomes would materially change this value.
caveats: The proxy spans industries and firm sizes.
Intentions, listings, and completed control transfers are different quantities.

q.rationale: A shop can retain benefits through faster response, higher booking conversion, denser mobile routes, fewer wrong-part orders, more technician utilization, and better claims documentation. Insurer fee schedules and network terms, consumer price shopping, software fees, technician wage pressure, rework liability, and required human review pass through or consume part of the gain.
q.change_evidence: Matched operator evidence on quote conversion, supplements, part errors, route density, technician hours, pricing, insurer reimbursement, rework, and software cost would materially change retained benefit.
caveats: No source isolates AI-created benefit from ordinary digitization or ADAS-related ticket growth.
Faster throughput may require additional calibration equipment and quality-control expense.

d5.bridge: FHWA's Spring 2025 baseline projects light-duty vehicle miles traveled growing 0.6% annually through 2043, implying roughly 3% more exposure over five years. I use that as the primary quantity anchor and allow modest additional support from a growing registered fleet and greater calibration content per replacement, while treating calibration complexity chiefly as quality rather than extra unit quantity. VMT can overstate glass demand if durability improves or weather and road-debris incidence falls; ADAS-equipped windshields, aging vehicles, and severe-weather exposure can make service demand grow faster.
d5.rationale: Vehicle use should edge higher and glass remains damage-driven, but weather, road conditions, glass design, repair-versus-replacement decisions, insurance deductibles, and quality adjustment create a wide range around modest real growth.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry counts of repairs, replacements, calibrations, mobile jobs, insurance claims, and constant-quality revenue by vehicle age would materially change the five-year ratio.
caveats: VMT is an exposure proxy, not a direct forecast of glass breakage.
ADAS recalibration increases service content but should not be counted mechanically as higher constant-quality demand.

o.rationale: Nearly all year-five quantity still requires an accountable operator to source the correct glass, remove and bond it safely, control adhesive cure and leaks, document the job, and perform or arrange manufacturer-required camera calibration. Better factory glass, do-it-yourself chip repair, dealer capture, and a small amount of automated inspection can displace independent-shop quantity but do not remove accountability from completed replacement work.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on robotic installation, OEM or dealer capture, do-it-yourself repair, remote calibration, and insurer-directed platform fulfillment would materially change operator need.
caveats: Operator need can remain high even if administrative labor per job falls substantially.
Mobile service changes the location of accountable work, not the need for it.

narrative.executive_view: Automotive glass is a coherent outsourced service with a digitizable claims, parts, scheduling, and routing layer wrapped around a physical and increasingly safety-critical installation. AI can improve throughput and coordination, but it does not remove the technician or calibration accountability from most jobs.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can handle intake, damage triage, estimate drafting, insurer documentation, VIN and part matching, customer communication, appointment changes, route suggestions, record checks, and technician knowledge retrieval. Humans remain responsible for physical glass work, vehicle protection, adhesive and cure control, leak checks, calibration setup and execution, and safety-significant exceptions.
narrative.value_capture: Benefits can appear as higher booking conversion, fewer part errors, denser mobile routes, and more technician jobs per day. Insurer reimbursement constraints, transparent consumer quotes, software cost, technician pay, calibration investment, and rework liability cap retention.
narrative.firm_availability: Most stand-alone glass firms fit the external-service screen, but the supplied target count is small and margin-derived. Captive work, micro mobile operators, insurer concentration, multi-location ownership, and technician or owner dependence reduce the number of transferable firms.
narrative.demand_durability: Vehicle travel offers a modestly positive anchor, while glass damage remains weather- and incident-sensitive. More ADAS-equipped windshields support sophisticated service content, but that complexity is not treated as pure unit growth under a constant-quality demand measure.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainties are the broad occupational proxy, lack of exact job-volume data, unpredictable storm and road-debris incidence, insurer price control, calibration quality adjustment, and conversion of broad succession intentions into completed external control transfers.
narrative.principal_driver: Digitized claims intake, part matching, scheduling, and mobile routing can raise technician utilization and reduce avoidable job friction.
narrative.principal_weakness: Physical installation and calibration remain safety-critical, while the qualifying firm population is small and the demand evidence is indirect.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 811122 Automotive Glass Replacement Shops
sources.supports: Official activity definition and classification boundaries used for the lens and operator-need reasoning.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 811120
sources.supports: Five-digit body, paint, interior, and glass occupational mix used as the task-exposure proxy.

sources.title: ADAS Recalibration
sources.supports: Operator evidence on insurance coordination, mobile service, static and dynamic calibration, technician work, and manufacturer-required recalibration after replacement.

sources.title: 2025 FHWA Forecasts of Vehicle Miles Traveled
sources.supports: Light-duty vehicle-miles-traveled growth used as the primary five-year demand proxy.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census: Other Services Basic Statistics
sources.supports: Exact-industry establishment and revenue context used to qualify firm availability and classification coherence.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Employer-owner five-year sale and transfer intentions used as the broad starting point for the qualifying-control-transfer proxy.
